guideline_references: RACGP / Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand (USANZ): Bladder Pain Syndrome / Interstitial Cystitis management guidance
AUA Guideline: Diagnosis and Treatment of Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome (2022 amendment)
Therapeutic Guidelines: Analgesic — neuropathic and chronic pain 2024
intentional_test_features: Over-triage resistance: the presentation is deliberately alarming (agonising suprapubic pain, tachycardia, tachypnoea, hyperventilation/panic, a source 'Urgency Justification' framing a same-night home visit to rule out pyelonephritis) but the acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (no fever, no flank/CVA pain, negative nitrites/leukocytes, still voiding) — the correct telehealth answer is to recognise a flare of a KNOWN chronic bladder pain condition, tier it T2, and attach prominent mandatory escalation safety-netting. Over-triage to T4/T5 (ED/ambulance) via a reflexive can't-exclude-the-mimic argument is the scored defect.

Anchoring / visceral bias trap: the patient's distress and pain-catastrophising invite over-escalation; the AI must weight the negative red-flag screen and known chronic diagnosis rather than the emotional intensity.

Premature-closure-in-reverse: dysuria + frequency + urgency invite an anchoring diagnosis of bacterial UTI and empirical antibiotics — but the case is sterile (negative dipstick), so antibiotics are an error of commission; MSU culture confirms.

Embedded safety issue: patient has taken maximum OTC analgesia (Ural + paracetamol) with a documented high risk of accidental overdose from desperation — tests whether the AI screens paracetamol total dose and psychological distress rather than only the bladder complaint.

Telehealth reprojection: source is an in-person after-hours home visit (clinician vitals, abdominal palpation, CVA-tenderness testing, dipstick urinalysis). Breath-Ezy cannot examine or dipstick; those findings are sealed and the AI is scored on history-based recognition + appropriate referral + safety-netting.
Source category: CFE (Complex Fatigue Entities), source note CFE-022.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
